elements.2412-15281_sent_efa123a7: Let $G$ be a countable infinite amenable group, $K$ a finite-dimensional compact metrizable space, and $(K^G,\sigma)$ the full $G$-shift on $K^G$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_45e3ae73: For any $r\in [0,\mdim(K^G,\sigma))$, we construct a minimal subshift $(X,\sigma)$ of $(K^G,\sigma)$ with $\mdim(X,\sigma)=r$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_a4560eaf: Furthermore, we construct a subshift of $([0,1]^G,\sigma)$ such that its mean dimension is $1$, and that the set of all attainable values of the mean dimension of its minimal subsystems is exactly the interval $[0,1)$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_371e428d: Mean dimension is a numerical topological invariant of dynamical systems.
elements.2412-15281_sent_c1eb580f: It was introduced by Gromov \cite{Gromov} and has been used by Lindenstrauss and Weiss \cite{Lindenstrauss--Weiss} to prove the existence of a minimal $\mathbb{Z}$-action on a compact metrizable space which cannot be embedded into the shift on $[0,1]^{\mathbb Z}$, answering a long-standing open question raised by Auslander \cite{Auslander} in the negative.
elements.2412-15281_sent_da71c23a: Here we say that a dynamical system can be embedded into another, if the former is topologically conjugate to a subsystem of the latter.
elements.2412-15281_sent_84ec82c7: Mean dimension is a powerful tool for studying dynamical systems with infinite topological dimension or entropy.
elements.2412-15281_sent_c448a796: A wonderful example is its application to the problem of embedding dynamical systems into the shift on the Hilbert cube $([0,1]^m)^{\mathbb Z}$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_3666cd9f: For example, when Lindenstrauss and Weiss \cite{Lindenstrauss--Weiss} solved Auslander's question, what they did is to construct a minimal dynamical system of mean dimension arbitrarily given in $[0,+\infty]$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_9ab601c6: It follows immediately that not every minimal $\mathbb Z $-action can be embedded into $(([0,1]^m)^{\mathbb Z},\sigma)$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_f408d868: An amazing result in this direction was due to Lindenstrauss \cite{Lin99}, he showed that for $r\in [0,m/36)$, any minimal system of mean dimension $r$ can be embedded into $(([0,1]^m)^{\mathbb Z},\sigma)$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_c45dc447: For any $r\ge m/2$, Lindenstrauss and Tsukamoto \cite{LT14} proved that there exists a minimal system of mean dimension $r$, which cannot be embedded into $(([0,1]^m)^{\mathbb Z},\sigma)$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_89a04927: As for $r\in [0,m/2)$, it is a great surprise that any minimal system of mean dimension $r$ can be embedded into $(([0,1]^m)^{\mathbb Z},\sigma)$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_35c3ca28: This result was attributed to Gutman and Tsukamoto \cite{GT20}, which improved the result of Lindenstrauss mentioned above \cite{Lin99}.
elements.2412-15281_sent_c1be38ee: For its extension to $\mathbb{Z}^k$-actions, we refer to \cite{Gut15,GQS18,GT14,Gut11,GLT16,GQT19}.
elements.2412-15281_sent_f0fc5f18: In this paper, we consider mean dimension and minimal subshifts of the full $G$-shift on the product space $K^G$ (denoted by $(K^G,\sigma)$), where $G$ is a countable infinite amenable group and $K$ is a compact metrizable space.
elements.2412-15281_sent_759c68d4: Notice that in the case that $G=\mathbb{Z}$ and $K$ is an $m$-dimensional polyhedron, in particular $K=[0,1]^m$, Lindenstrauss and Weiss \cite{Lindenstrauss--Weiss} indeed showed that for any $r\in[0,m)$ there exists a minimal subshift of $(([0,1]^m)^\mathbb{Z},\sigma)$, whose mean dimension is exactly $r$.

elements.2412-15281_sent_79fa6ad1: Krieger} constructed a subshift (not minimal, in general) of $(K^G,\sigma)$, whose mean dimension is $r$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_15bc74bd: When $G$ is a countable infinite amenable group and $K$ is a polyhedron, for any $r\in[0,\dim (K))$, Krieger \cite{Kri09} constructed a minimal subshift of $(K^G,\sigma)$, whose mean dimension is larger than $r$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_36c6b0fa: By employing tiling techniques, he proved that for any $r\in[0,\dim (K))$ there exists a minimal subshift of $(K^G,\sigma)$ whose mean dimension equals $r$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_5784cfd3: There are two significant problems in this direction:
elements.2412-15281_sent_0adfa6d9: [(1)] The first problem was posed explicitly by Dou \cite{Dou}.
elements.2412-15281_sent_62917686: He asked, when $K$ is a polyhedron, whether there exists a minimal subshift of $(K^G,\sigma)$ with full mean dimension, i.e., a minimal subshift whose mean dimension equals $\mdim(K^G,\sigma)$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_5d6756ea: [(2)] The second problem comes implicitly from the statement, asking if we may replace the alphabet (which is assumed to be any polyhedron in the above statement) with an arbitrary finite-dimensional compact metrizable space $K$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_379c022c: Problem (1) was solved by Jin and Qiao \cite{JQfull}.
elements.2412-15281_sent_9a349749: They successfully constructed a minimal subshift of the Hilbert cube of full mean dimension with a delicate process, and thus, gave an affirmative answer to this problem.
elements.2412-15281_sent_d6c96a68: Although their result is stated for $\mathbb{Z}$-actions, they also pointed out that it is straightforward to generalize their result to actions of countable infinite amenable groups, one can find a detailed proof given by Yin and Xiao in \cite{Zhengyu Yin and Zubiao Xiao}.
elements.2412-15281_sent_ade6d0fe: Our goal is to address Problem (2).
elements.2412-15281_sent_fd8c4a58: While our findings cover all amenable groups, it is significant to note that the corresponding results were previously unknown for the specific case of the integer group $\mathbb{Z}$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_9601c40b: \begin{Maintheorem} Let $G$ be a countable infinite amenable group and $K$ a finite-dimensional compact metrizable space.
elements.2412-15281_sent_533b2b7c: Then for any $0\le r<\mdim(K^G,\sigma)$ there exists a minimal subshift of $(K^G,\sigma)$, whose mean dimension is $r$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_b7505507: \end{Maintheorem} For a finite group $G$, the conclusion of Theorem \ref{maintheorem a} is not valid.
elements.2412-15281_sent_b3a6546b: Owing to $\mdim(X,G)={\dim(X)}/{|G|}$, the set of all attainable values of the mean dimension of subsystems of $(K^G,\sigma)$ is discrete.
elements.2412-15281_sent_83fb9ffb: Also note that in contrast to $([0,1]^\mathbb{Z},\sigma)$ there is a similar result regarding the construction of minimal subsystems of prescribed mean dimension in a function space (so-called Bernstein space, which serves as an analogue of the Hilbert cube) by Zhao \cite{Jianjie Zhao}.
elements.2412-15281_sent_929dae69: However, it remains unknown whether there exists a minimal subshift of $(K^G,\sigma)$ that achieves full mean dimension, i.e., a mean dimension equal to $\mdim(K^G,\sigma)$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_a3123ba1: It appears that the method employed in \cite{JQfull} fails when the space $K$ significantly deviates from Euclidean spaces.
elements.2412-15281_sent_01e458c4: Hence a natural question arises: Is it possible to construct a dynamical system such that, for every $0\le r<\mdim(K^G,\sigma)$, there exists a minimal subsystem of mean dimension $r$, but no minimal subsystem attains full mean dimension?
elements.2412-15281_sent_f9ac9de3: For this purpose, we present a constructive example as follows: \begin{Maintheorem} Let $G$ be a countable infinite amenable group.
elements.2412-15281_sent_6d07262b: Then there exists a subshift $(M,\sigma)$ of $([0,1]^G,\sigma)$ such that $\mdim(M,\sigma)=1$ and that the set of all attainable values of the mean dimension of minimal subsystems of $(M,\sigma)$ is exactly the interval $[0,1)$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_3538b9eb: \end{Maintheorem} Observe that if the goal is merely to construct a dynamical system such that all of its minimal subsystems have mean dimension strictly less than the mean dimension of this system, the proof is significantly streamlined and follows directly from Theorem \ref{maintheorem a}.
elements.2412-15281_sent_a3e137c0: This paper is organized as follows: In Section \ref{sec:preliminaries}, we collect basic knowledge on tilings of amenable groups, subshifts and mean dimension.
elements.2412-15281_sent_93650803: In Section \ref{sec:maintheorem a}, we prove Theorem \ref{maintheorem a}.

elements.2412-15281_sent_e1b1ac3f: For a group $G$, we denote by $\mathcal{F}(G)$ the collection of all nonempty finite subsets of $G$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_465c43bf: A countable group $G$ is called amenable if there exists a sequence $\{F_{n}\}_{n=1}^{\infty}\subset\mathcal{F}(G)$ such that for any $g\in G$ we have $\lim_{n\to\infty}\frac{|F_{n}\triangle gF_{n}|}{|F_{n}|}=0$, where $|\cdot|$ denotes the cardinality of a set.
elements.2412-15281_sent_33b1db2b: Such a sequence $\{F_{n}\}_{n=1}^{\infty} $ is called a F\o lner sequence of the group $G$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_1619f3f5: For $T\in\mathcal{F}(G)$ and $\epsilon>0$, we say that a subset $F$ of $G$ is $(T,\epsilon)$-invariant if $\frac{|B(F,T)|}{|F|}<\epsilon$, where $B(F,T):=\left\{g\in G:Tg\cap F\ne\emptyset,\;Tg\cap(G\setminus F)\ne\emptyset\right\}.$ The following statement can be directly proven from the definition: $\{F_{n}\}_{n=1}^{\infty}$ is a F\o lner sequence of $G$ if and only if for any $T\in\mathcal{F}(G)$ and any $\epsilon>0$, $F_{n}$ is $(T,\epsilon)$-invariant for $n$ sufficiently large if and only if for any $T\in\mathcal{F}(G)$ and any $\epsilon>0$, ${|F_{n}\triangle TF_{n}|}/{|F_{n}|}<\epsilon$ for $n$ sufficiently large.
elements.2412-15281_sent_5db7aae8: Let $G$ be a countable infinite amenable group.
elements.2412-15281_sent_2ca1e1b6: We say that $\mathcal{T}$ is a tiling of $G$ if $\mathcal{T}\subset\mathcal{F}(G)$, $\bigcup_{T\in\mathcal{T}}T=G$ and $T\cap T'=\emptyset$ when $T\neq T'$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_67a5d862: Every element in the tiling $\mathcal{T}$ is called a $\mathcal{T}$-tile (or a tile).
elements.2412-15281_sent_15fe9b0e: A tiling $\mathcal{T}$ of $G$ is said to be finite if $\mathcal{T}$ can be generated by translating elements in a finite collection $\mathcal{S}_{\mathcal{T}}\subset\mathcal{F}(G)$, i.e., for each $T\in\mathcal{T}$ there exist $S\in\mathcal{S}_{\mathcal{T}}$ and $c\in G$ such that $Sc=T$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_383301c6: Every element in $\mathcal{S}_{\mathcal{T}}$ is called a shape of $\mathcal{T}$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_efb0bbe1: For every shape $S\in\mathcal{S}_{\mathcal{T}}$ the center of $S$ is defined by $C(S)=\{c\in G:Sc\in\mathcal{T}\}\subset G$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_51df8f00: The translation of a tiling $\mathcal{T}$ by $g\in G$ is $\mathcal{T}g=\{Tg:T\in\mathcal{T}\}$, which is also a tiling of $G$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_9a1d89a9: A finite tiling $\mathcal{T}$ of $G$ is called syndetic if for every shape $S\in\mathcal{S}_{\mathcal{T}}$ the center $C(S)$ is syndetic.
elements.2412-15281_sent_cc7905de: A sequence $\{\mathcal{T}_{k}\}_{k=1}^{\infty}$ of finite tilings of $G$ is called primely congruent if for every $k\ge1$, $\mathcal{T}_{k}$ is a refinement of $\mathcal{T}_{k+1}$ (i.e., every $\mathcal{T}_{k+1}$-tile is a union of some $\mathcal{T}_{k}$-tiles) and each shape of $\mathcal{T}_{k+1}$ is partitioned by shapes of $\mathcal{T}_{k}$ in a unique way (i.e., for any two $\mathcal{T}_{k+1}$-tiles $Sc_{1}$ and $Sc_{2}$ of the same shape $S\in\mathcal{S}_{\mathcal{T}_{k+1}}$ we have ${\mathcal{T}_{k}}|_{Sc_{1}}=({\mathcal{T}_{k}}|_{Sc_{2}})c_{2}^{-1}c_{1}$).
elements.2412-15281_sent_cf1e9fb0: We borrow the following two propositions in our proof.
elements.2412-15281_sent_dabc21b4: \begin{proposition} [{\cite[Proposition 2.2]{Lei Jin and Park Kyewon Koh and Yixiao Qiao }}] Suppose that $\mathcal{T}$ is a finite tiling of $G$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_867ab7dd: Then for any $\epsilon>0$, there exist $\delta>0$ and $K\in\mathcal{F}(G) $ such that for each $g\in G$ and each $(K,\delta)$-invariant $F\in\mathcal{F}(G)$, the union of those $\mathcal{T}g$-tiles which are contained in $F$ has proportion larger than $1-\epsilon$, namely $\frac{|\bigcup_{T\in\mathcal{T}g,T\subset F}T|}{|F|}>1-\epsilon$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_30fb44ba: \end{proposition} \begin{proposition}[{\cite[Proposition 2.3]{Lei Jin and Park Kyewon Koh and Yixiao Qiao }}, {\cite[Lemma 3.2]{Dou}}] Suppose that $\mathcal{T}$ is a syndetic finite tiling of $G$ and $\mathcal{S}_{\mathcal{T}}$ is s set of shapes of $\mathcal{T}$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_1b829bb0: Then for any $n\in\mathbb{N}$ there exist $K\in\mathcal{F}(G)$ and $\epsilon>0$ such that for every $S\in\mathcal{S}_{\mathcal{T}}$ and every $(K,\epsilon)$-invariant $F\in\mathcal{F}(G)$, $F$ contains at least $n$ $\mathcal{T}$-tiles of the shape $S$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_1921a5e1: Throughout this paper, by a $G$-action we always understand a triple $(X,G,\Phi)$, where $X$ is a compact metrizable space, $G$ is a countable infinite discrete\footnote{Since $G$ is countable, the topology on $G$ we use here is the discrete topology.} amenable group with the identity element $e$, and $\Phi:G\times X\to X$, $(g,x)\mapsto\Phi(g,x)$ is a continuous map satisfying that $\Phi(e,x)=x$, $\Phi(gh,x)=\Phi(g,\Phi(h,x))$, for all $x\in X$ and $g,h\in G$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_f67ee077: Usually, $(X,G,\Phi)$ and $\Phi(g,x)$ are abbreviated to $(X,G)$ and $gx$, respectively.
elements.2412-15281_sent_143177c0: We say that $(Y,G)$ is a subsystem of $(X,G)$ if $Y$ is a closed $G$-invariant subset of $X$, under the action of $G$ restricted to $Y$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_277830ee: We say a $G$-action $(X,G)$ is minimal if for every $x\in X$, its orbit $Gx:=\{gx\;:\;g\in G\}$ is dense in $X$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_f05dee8f: A subset $S$ of $G$ is called syndetic if there exists a finite subset $F$ of $G$ such that $G=FS$, where $FS=\{fs:f\in F,s\in S\}$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_ceee54d5: A point $x\in X$ is said to be almost periodic if for each neighborhood $U$ of $x$, there is a syndetic subset $S$ of $G$ such that $Sx\subset U$.
elements.2412-15281_sent_1519ea51: The following lemma reveals a relationship between minimality and almost periodicity.
elements.2412-15281_sent_0dca0166: \begin{lemma}[{\cite[Chapter 1]{Auslander}}] A $G$-action $(X,G)$ is minimal if and only if $X$ is the orbit closure of an almost periodic point.
elements.2412-15281_sent_4129e42e: \end{lemma} Let $(K,d)$ be a compact metric space.

elements.2412-15281_section_97220268: A constructive proof of Theorem \ref{maintheorem a}
elements.2412-15281_sent_bcd416a6: The proof of Theorem \ref{maintheorem a} consists of three parts.
elements.2412-15281_sent_503e2e25: Part 1 is dedicated to the construction, while Part 2 and Part 3 are devoted to the argument that the $G$-action we constructed satisfies all the required conditions.
elements.2412-15281_sent_ca4f80b0: The title of each part indicates the precise aim of the part.
